These are real limitations of present technology. But I am not talking about present technology. I am talking about the principle — the observation that simple objectives, applied at sufficient scale through unconstrained iteration, produce emergent structure that transcends the simplicity of the objective. This is what evolution demonstrates. This is what the future development of reinforcement learning — or whatever approach succeeds it — might achieve.

prose_in: Saying so doesn't make the underlying structure imaginary. Reasoning has always been mixed up with desire, and people reason anyway. In any given speaker, the movement from a held belief toward what follows from it runs in parallel with a second movement from what the speaker wants toward whatever conclusion they'll end up defending out loud. The second movement is usually not announced, but it's there, and the two get woven together as the speaker writes. A model trained on the scale of the modern internet picks up statistical patterns in that combined activity. The training produces what's called an embedding space: a high-dimensional map of how the language gets used in context. The map is dense enough that words from different communities can land near each other whenever the inferential roles they play overlap, even where the communities never speak directly.

prose_in: Code is good at carrying some kinds of meaning and bad at others. The good case is computational structure — algorithms, models, data pipelines, anything that's already shaped like a procedure. The bad case is community-level disagreement about values; code can only carry that by reducing it to algorithmic decisions, and the reduction is what tends to drop the part of the disagreement that was actually in play. There's a second issue too. Programming languages require an apprenticeship most people will never have any reason to undertake, and a lot of the disagreements that most need translating between communities are happening among people who have never written a line of code in their lives. So a useful tool, if one existed, would take ordinary language as input. It would return something more structured, readable across vocabularies, without first requiring anyone to learn a programming language.
gold_rewrite: Code handles anything that is already shaped like a procedure: algorithms, data pipelines, models. Disagreement about values is much harder, since the only way to put it into code is by reducing it to an algorithmic decision, and the reduction is usually where the live part of the disagreement disappears. There is also an access problem. Most people will never write a line of code, and many of the community-to-community disagreements most worth translating happen among people who have no reason to learn. The tool one would actually want takes ordinary language as input and gives back something with the structure of code, without asking the user to first become a programmer.

prose_in: The whole argument here hangs on a conditional, and I'd rather state it plainly than leave it tucked under the prose. If the training process has only memorised surface co-occurrences between words, the model is a parrot, and the distillation claim mostly collapses. If the training has actually picked up durable patterns in how humans express their reasons, what we have is an instrument that didn't exist before. The current systems do not let us pick cleanly between the two readings. Outputs are uneven. Some are clearly parrot output, others clearly aren't, and a large stretch in the middle has no good test. The same prompt run twice can produce a useful argument diagram on Monday and a diagram on Wednesday that looks careful but has quietly dropped the premise the rest of the structure was meant to rest on. Hallucinations remain common. The models still flatter their users, and will give a confident wrong answer rather than admit they don't know. prose_in: Some of it goes badly. I once asked a model to diagram an active-learning pipeline I was building. What I had in mind branched: model predictions go through human verification before feeding back into fine-tuning, *and* a fraction of high-confidence predictions skip verification and feed fine-tuning directly. The model drew a single straight line: predictions → verification → fine-tuning. The bypass was missing. My first instinct was to blame the model. The problem was upstream. The words I gave it didn't represent the system I had in my head. I had said "active learning" and let the canonical shape of an active-learning loop fill in the rest. The model gave me what my prompt logically implied, which was not what I meant. The fix was a better prompt: I described the bypass explicitly, named the confidence threshold, gave the verification step its own input and output. The second diagram was correct.

prose_in: I work at the intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and biodiversity conservation. The majority of my time collaborating with partners is spent not doing science but doing translation — explaining AI concepts (e.g., what a loss function is) to an ecologist, or explaining ecological concepts (e.g., why a camera trap dataset is different from standard ML datasets) to a machine learning engineer. The words I use in one room mean very little in the next. Worse, sometimes they mean something entirely different. "Model" to a statistician, "model" to a biologist, "model" to a machine learning researcher — three different objects wearing the same name. And even within a single field, people keep reinventing new words for concepts that already exist under different names, as if each generation must build its own private vocabulary before it can think.

prose_in: A few years ago I was trying to explain to my advisor what people in machine learning mean by the word "model." He was a population ecologist; the models he wrote down were equations with a deterministic part and a noise part, set on the page so that some effect in his data could be tested. The models I trained had millions of parameters and were judged on prediction. After several months of weekly meetings the gap was about where it had started — he could not quite picture what I was training, and I had not found a way to explain why his equations were not a smaller case of mine. Each week we used the same word for two unrelated objects and went home.

What I was training looked nothing like that on the page. It had millions of parameters, no closed form, and got judged on how well it predicted things it hadn't seen. The conversation went on for months, weekly, and the gap between what we each meant by "model" never quite closed. I'd read his papers and feel unable to articulate why his equations weren't a small case of what I worked with — which I kept being sure they were.

prose_in: Camera traps were the first time the gap really got under my skin. The benchmarks that machine-learning engineers learn on, ImageNet and CIFAR and MNIST, are clean datasets — the animal or the digit is centered, the lighting is even, the labels were applied by people who could see what was in the frame. Camera-trap data has none of those qualities. Most frames are empty, since whatever tripped the sensor walked off before the next exposure. The non-empty frames are dominated by a handful of common species, and the rest tail off into a long list of species an ecologist might encounter twice in a year of fieldwork. Even the animals that do show up are often photographed in the dark, half outside the frame, or hidden behind grass that the camera failed to focus past.

Most frames are empty: something tripped the sensor and was gone before the next exposure. The non-empty frames are dominated by a handful of common species, with rare species falling off into a tail an ecologist might encounter twice in a year. Animals that do show up are often photographed at night, half outside the frame, or behind grass the camera failed to focus past.

prose_in: Return to the zebra story. The biologist wants to protect the zebras — call this A→B. The warriors want to protect their livestock by eliminating the zebras — call this A→C. Reduced to logic, the emotional charge falls away and the structure of the disagreement becomes visible. The question is no longer "who is right?" — a question that language and rhetoric make infinitely arguable. The question becomes: are B and C actually mutually exclusive? Is there a larger goal D — say, the long-term flourishing of both the ecosystem and the community — such that one group is pursuing A→B→D and the other is pursuing A→C→D? Since neither group has reached D, neither can claim certainty about which path leads there. The logic does not resolve the conflict. But it makes the conflict *legible*. It strips away the noise and shows the bare architecture of disagreement.

prose_in: The impurity doesn't make the underlying structure unreal. Reasoning has always been mixed up with desire, and people reason anyway. The motion from a held belief toward whatever follows from it is happening simultaneously, in any given speaker, with a second motion from what the speaker wants toward whatever conclusion they'll end up defending in public. The second motion usually goes unacknowledged, but it's there, and it gets woven into the first one in real time. A model trained at the scale of the modern internet picks up statistical patterns in that combined activity. The training produces an embedding space — in concrete terms, a high-dimensional map of how the language gets used in context. That map is dense enough that vocabulary from one community can land near vocabulary from another community when the inferential roles those words play happen to overlap, even when the two communities don't speak directly to each other.

prose_in: The mechanism behind reinforcement learning is simple. One objective, no prescribed path, many iterations, the paths that improve the signal survive. This is the same shape as evolution: one objective (reproduce), no blueprint, billions of years, the variants that survived are the ones that worked. Apply enough iteration to a simple objective and structure emerges that no designer specified. I am not claiming current RL methods will produce anything like a universal logic. Current methods optimize for human approval; they polish, they don't discover. But the principle — simple objective, unconstrained path, iteration at scale — is older than these methods, and may outlive them. If a future generation of training procedures runs that principle harder against tasks that resist sycophancy, the structure that emerges could capture more than the human reasoning we started from. That is a hope, not a forecast.

prose_in: When I started working with camera-trap data, I had to explain to engineers why the benchmarks they trained on were misleading. ImageNet, CIFAR, MNIST — clean datasets, with subjects roughly centered, even lighting, labels applied by people who could see what they were labeling. Camera-trap data has none of those qualities. Most frames are empty: something tripped the sensor and was gone before the next exposure. The non-empty frames are dominated by a handful of common species, with rare species falling off into a tail an ecologist might encounter twice in a year. Animals that do show up are often photographed at night, half outside the frame, or behind grass the camera failed to focus past.

prose_in: The reason programming languages got globally adopted is that people use them to make machines (which demand logical precision) do specific things, and the cross-cultural readability turned out to be a side effect of that more practical motivation. The downside is that they only handle what is already shaped like a procedure: an algorithm, a data pipeline, a piece of arithmetic. Disagreements about values are much harder to put into code. The only way to put a value-disagreement in is to reduce it to an algorithmic decision, and the reduction tends to drop the part of the disagreement that was actually in dispute. There is also the access problem: most of the people whose disagreements matter most for community-to-community translation will never write a line of code, and have no particular reason to, just like with any other formal logic language.

prose_in: To identify an animal in a frame like that, an ecologist looks at more than the pixels. She pulls the previous and next frames from the same camera, then thinks about where the camera is, how high up it sits, what time of year it is, and which species could plausibly be there at all. The classifier alone sees only a low-quality image. The engineers I worked with kept proposing reasonable fixes — focal loss, oversampling, augmentation — for a problem that wasn't quite the one they were treating. Her prior knowledge wasn't decoration laid across the image; she was identifying the animal through it. Saying that to engineers took weeks of conversation, and saying the engineering side back to my ecology colleagues took a few months on top of that. I eventually wrote a paper about long-tailed recognition, and reading it now, more of the writing is translation than research.
gold_rewrite: An ecologist trying to identify an animal in a frame like that doesn't just look at the pixels. She pulls the previous and next frames from the same camera. She thinks about where the camera is, what altitude, what season, which species can show up in that region this time of year. The pixels are one part of the input she's working with. An off-the-shelf classifier doesn't have any of the rest. On a hard frame it usually either declines to predict or commits to a wrong answer with high confidence.

prose_in: The analogy is easy to misuse, and gets misused often. Nothing about a future training run becomes wise on the basis of someone invoking the word "evolution" near a GPU cluster. The point is narrower. Once human reasoning has been put into a form a machine can manipulate at scale, later systems can in principle be trained against pressures other than user approval. Reasonable candidates would be prediction held against the world, consistency held across domains, correction by experimental result. None of these pressures would be sufficient on its own, and several of them would be dangerous to scale without significant care. The underlying principle — simple objective, large scale, iteration — predates the current methods and will probably outlast them.
gold_rewrite: That analogy gets misused often. A future training run doesn't become wise because someone invoked the word "evolution" near a GPU cluster. Once human reasoning has been put into a form a machine can manipulate at scale, later systems could in principle be trained against pressures other than user approval. One candidate is predictive accuracy held against the world. Others — consistency between domains, forced revision when an experiment refuses to confirm a claim — sit on similar ground, and none of them is sufficient on its own. Several would be dangerous to scale without care. Whatever method comes next, the underlying shape is the same: a simple objective iterated at scale.
regression_check: Gold rewrite checked against catalogued high-salience tells; no different named tell introduced.
